ctx.name: Riley
ctx.notes: Riley is in week two and is moving quickly through codebase orientation, but he has mostly learned from docs and async notes rather than direct team contact. I want to check whether he feels clear on ownership boundaries, incident expectations, and where to ask for context before making architecture assumptions. There is a risk he looks productive while building an internal map that differs from how the team actually operates. This check-in should identify missing context early and convert it into concrete onboarding support.

focusPoints.label: How he’s landing with the team day-to-day.
focusPoints.reason: In week two, he’s learned mostly via docs and async notes rather than direct contact. One possibility is he’s moving fast but missing the real operating texture—worth clarifying who he’s been working with, how often he’s getting live feedback, and what context he’s still not hearing.

focusPoints.label: Ownership boundaries and what “good” means.
focusPoints.reason: He may be building an internal map that differs from how the team actually operates. Whether his ownership boundaries and expectations are explicit enough for him to make safe calls without overstepping is worth clarifying early.

focusPoints.label: Where he needs context before assumptions.
focusPoints.reason: He’s at the stage where architecture assumptions can be made quickly from incomplete information. Ask what questions are still unanswered, which decisions feel risky, and where he’s currently waiting on clarity—so you can convert missing context into concrete onboarding support.

focusPoints.label: Incident expectations and escalation paths.
focusPoints.reason: Onboarding for a Mid backend engineer should include how incidents are handled in practice—what to monitor, when to escalate, and how to communicate hard news. Whether he knows the expectation for incident ownership and escalation early prevents “looks productive” drift into the wrong mental model.

transcript.question: What’s been most on your mind at work lately, Riley?
transcript.answer: I am comfortable reading the services, but I am less sure how decisions are really made here.

transcript.question: When you say 'how decisions are really made here,' what feels most unclear?
transcript.answer: I can trace most request paths now, though I still miss historical context behind certain tradeoffs.

transcript.question: Which tradeoffs feel hardest to read without the backstory?
transcript.answer: I have not been fully clear on who is on point for each domain when priorities conflict.

transcript.question: Where do the ownership boundaries still feel fuzzy to you?
transcript.answer: I am trying not to interrupt people, so sometimes I keep questions in a notes doc instead of asking.

transcript.question: What kinds of questions are sitting in that notes doc right now?
transcript.answer: I would benefit from a clearer guide for what to escalate quickly versus what to batch.

transcript.question: What would make the escalation line clearer in practice for you?
transcript.answer: The runbook helped, but some practical handoff details only show up during live incidents.

transcript.question: What are you still waiting to understand before you change an architecture assumption?
transcript.answer: A short weekly architecture review with someone senior would help me calibrate faster.

transcript.question: Who would you want to pull in first when you need context fast?
transcript.answer: If I know exactly what ownership I should take this month, I can contribute with more confidence.

transcript.question: What would make the next couple of weeks steadier for you?
transcript.answer: I will ask one architecture question in the next incident review instead of keeping it in my notes doc.
